sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, DOWN, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 22, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicking yellow dot did nothing. Both right-panel shapes are solid (no outlines). Green dot still in container at rows 38-39, cols 12-13. Level not complete.
- Goal model: Need to figure out what completes the level. Maybe the green dot needs to be moved to a specific position.
- Action model: Arrow keys move green dot within container. Clicking right-panel shapes removes outlines.
- Plan: Try moving the green dot to different positions within the container to see if it triggers something. Move LEFT first.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 21 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After LEFT, the green dot moved from cols 12-13 to cols 10-11. But after DOWN and UP, the green dot stayed at cols 10-11 (same row). So:
- LEFT moved the green dot from cols 12-13 to cols 10-11
- DOWN didn't move the green dot (still at rows 38-39)
- UP didn't move the green dot (still at rows 38-39)
Wait, but the code printed the green dot position AFTER each action, but I didn't re-query the segmentation after DOWN and UP. Let me check the current state.
Actually, looking at the code, I queried `seg` only once at the beginning, and then printed the green dot position three times using the same `seg` object. That's a bug - I should have re-queried after each action.
But the output shows:
- After LEFT: rows=38-39, cols=10-11 (moved left)
- After DOWN: rows=38-39, cols=10-11 (same - but this is from the old seg!)
- After UP: rows=38-39, cols=10-11 (same - but this is from the old seg!)
So I can't tell if DOWN and UP actually moved the green dot. Let me check the current state properly.
Looking at the current image, the green dot appears to be at the bottom-left of the container (rows 40-41, cols 10-11 maybe?). Let me check the current segmentation.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: LEFT moved green dot from cols 12-13 to cols 10-11. DOWN and UP results unclear due to code bug. Need to check current green dot position.
Goal model: Still unclear. Need to explore green dot movement and find completion condition.
Action model: Arrow keys move green dot within container. LEFT works, need to verify DOWN/UP/RIGHT.
Plan: Check current green dot position, then explore all possible positions within the container.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current green dot position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

print("Current green dot position:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
        break

# Check container area
print("\nContainer area (rows 37-44, cols 7-14):")
for r in range(37, 44):
    print(f"  Row {r}: {ascii_lines[r][7:14]}")

# Try moving RIGHT to see if it moves
result = action(['RIGHT'])
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print(f"\nAfter RIGHT: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Green dot: rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
        break

# Try moving DOWN
result = action(['DOWN'])
seg3 = current_frame.segmentation
print(f"\nAfter DOWN: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
for n in seg3['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Green dot: rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
        break

# Try moving UP
result = action(['UP'])
seg4 = current_frame.segmentation
print(f"\nAfter UP: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
for n in seg4['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  Green dot: rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
